title: Single-Wall and Double-Wall Exposure Techniques
narrationScript: Walk up to any weld with a source in one hand and a cassette in the other and you face the same first question every time. How many walls of material will the beam pass through, and how many of those walls am I actually trying to read? Answer that and the technique practically names itself. This lesson covers the family of single wall and double wall exposure techniques, when each applies, and the numbers that go with them. Single wall radiography is the baseline and the preference. The source sits on one side, the film sits on the other, and the beam penetrates exactly one wall of material. A plate butt weld is the obvious case. So is a large vessel you can enter, with film hung inside and the source outside, or the reverse. On pipe, single wall means getting either the film or the source inside, with the source inside and film wrapped outside being the common arrangement. Single wall gives the best image quality because the radiation only crosses the thickness you are interpreting, the image quality indicator is selected for that one wall, and exposure times stay short. The working rule across the codes is simple. Use a single wall technique whenever it is practical, and reach for double wall only when access forces you to. Now suppose the pipe is welded into a system and nobody is getting inside. The beam has to pass through both walls, and the choice becomes what you intend to view. For larger pipe, commonly above three and a half inches outside diameter, we use double wall exposure with single wall viewing. The source sits outside against or near one wall, the film is wrapped on the opposite side of the pipe, and only the weld next to the film is interpreted, because that image is sharp while the source side wall is blurred and enlarged. The source is offset slightly from the weld plane, or angled, so the image of the source side portion of the weld does not fall on top of the film side image you are reading. Since each shot only covers a portion of the circumference with acceptable quality, complete coverage takes at least three exposures spaced one hundred twenty degrees apart, and thicker walls or tighter quality levels can require more. On small bore pipe, three and a half inches outside diameter and under is the common threshold, there is not enough room to make that work, so we read both walls at once. That is double wall exposure with double wall viewing. The classic version is the elliptical technique. Offset the source along the axis of the pipe, and a little to the side, so the source side weld image is thrown clear of the film side image. On the radiograph the weld opens up into an ellipse, with the film side portion forming the bottom of the ellipse and the source side portion forming the top. Both walls are evaluated on the same film. Coverage takes at least two exposures, with the second rotated ninety degrees from the first, so the sections of weld that were viewed edge on in the first shot get viewed favorably in the second. When the geometry will not give you a clean ellipse, perhaps the weld reinforcement is wide, the joint is next to a fitting, or the offset throws the images into each other, use the superimposed technique instead. Shoot straight through both walls so the two weld images land on top of each other, and make at least three exposures, rotated sixty degrees or one hundred twenty degrees apart depending on the code wording, so that every portion of the weld is presented acceptably somewhere in the set. With every double wall technique, remember what the geometry costs you. The beam crosses two wall thicknesses, so exposures run longer. The source side image is magnified and less sharp. The image quality indicator selection and the density requirements follow the rules of the governing code for the wall being viewed. And location markers, lead numbers and letters laid against the part, must map every film to its position on the circumference, with enough overlap between successive exposures that no portion of the weld escapes coverage. Plan the shot count before the first exposure, mark the pipe into equal segments, and the set of films will tile the full weld with nothing missing. Choose single wall when you can, double wall single view on big pipe, ellipse or superimposed on small bore. That decision tree covers nearly every pipe weld you will ever shoot.
imagePrompt: A radiographer straps a film cassette against the far side of a large-bore steel pipe butt weld while positioning a collimated gamma projector source guide tube against the near wall on a fabrication shop floor.

sections.heading: The First Question: Walls Penetrated, Walls Viewed
sections.body:
Every exposure technique is named by two numbers: how many walls the beam **penetrates** and how many walls you intend to **view**. Get those straight and the rest is geometry.

- **Single-wall (SW):** beam crosses one wall; you view that wall. Best quality, first choice.
- **Double-wall exposure, single-wall viewing (DWSI):** beam crosses both walls of a pipe; you interpret only the wall **nearest the film**.
- **Double-wall exposure, double-wall viewing (DWDI):** beam crosses both walls and you interpret **both** on the same film — the small-bore technique, shot elliptical or superimposed.

The penetrated thickness sets the exposure; the viewed wall sets the IQI selection and quality requirements under the governing code. The universal rule: **use single-wall whenever it is practical**, and accept a double-wall technique only when access leaves no choice.

sections.heading: Single-Wall Radiography
sections.body:
In single-wall radiography the source is on one side of the part, the film is in contact with the opposite side, and the radiation penetrates exactly one wall thickness. Plate butt welds are shot this way as a matter of course. Vessels and tanks large enough to enter are shot single-wall with film inside and source outside, or source inside and film outside. On piping, single-wall means getting film or source **inside** the pipe: an internal crawler or a centered source with film wrapped around the outside (the panoramic case covered in the next lesson), or internal film cassettes against the wall with the source outside.

Why it is preferred:

- The beam crosses only the thickness being interpreted — **shortest exposure, least scatter buildup**.
- The IQI is selected for the single-wall thickness, and achieving the required sensitivity is easiest.
- Geometry is clean: source-to-film distance is measured directly, and geometric unsharpness is controlled with the full SFD working for you.

When a procedure or code asks why you shot a weld double-wall, the only acceptable answer is that single-wall access was not practical.

sections.caption: The three pipe-weld exposure families: single-wall, double-wall with single-wall viewing (source offset, three or more shots), and double-wall double-image for small bore.

sections.heading: DWSI: Double-Wall Exposure, Single-Wall Viewing
sections.body:
When a pipe is closed to internal access and the bore is too large for double-image work — commonly **above 3.5 in. OD** — the standard answer is DWSI. The source is placed **outside** the pipe, against or near one wall, and the film is wrapped against the **opposite** wall. The beam penetrates both walls, but only the **film-side weld image is interpreted**: it lies close to the film, so it is sharp and essentially unmagnified, while the source-side wall image is enlarged and blurred beyond use.

Two setup details make or break the shot:

- **Offset or angle the source** slightly from the weld plane so the unusable source-side weld image does not superimpose on the film-side image you are reading. With the source close to the near wall, the source-side image spreads so much it fades into background.
- **Plan coverage.** Each exposure presents only a limited arc of the circumference at acceptable quality, so complete coverage requires a **minimum of three exposures spaced 120° apart** — more for heavy walls or tight quality levels. Lead location markers at marked stations map every film to its arc, with deliberate overlap between adjacent shots so no weld length goes unexamined.

sections.heading: DWDI: Viewing Two Walls at Once
sections.body:
On small-bore pipe — **3.5 in. OD and under** is the common threshold — the walls are so close together that both project sharply, and there is no room to favor one. So both walls are exposed and **both are viewed on the same film**: double-wall exposure, double-wall (double-image) viewing.

The preferred form is the **elliptical technique**. The source is offset **along the pipe axis** (and typically slightly to the side) so the source-side weld image is thrown clear of the film-side image. On the radiograph the weld opens into an **ellipse**: the film-side weld forms one limb and the source-side weld the other, with the root regions separated for clean interpretation. Complete coverage requires **at least two exposures, rotated 90° to each other**, so weld sections viewed edge-on in the first shot are presented favorably in the second.

When geometry defeats the ellipse — wide reinforcement, adjacent fittings, pipe-to-flange joints, or images that overlap no matter the offset — fall back to the **superimposed technique**: shoot straight through the weld so the two wall images land on top of each other, and make **at least three exposures at 60° or 120° increments** so every portion of weld is presented acceptably somewhere in the set.

sections.caption: Elliptical technique geometry: offsetting the source along the pipe axis separates the source-side and film-side weld images into an open ellipse on the film.

sections.heading: Markers, Overlap, and Identification
sections.body:
Whatever the technique, the set of films must **prove** complete coverage, and that proof is built at setup, not at the viewer.

- **Location markers** — lead numbers and letters taped against the part at laid-out stations — image on every film and tie it to its arc of the weld. Marker placement (source side versus film side) follows the governing code's rules so the marker images demonstrate, not just suggest, coverage.
- **Overlap** between adjacent exposures is deliberate: the marker images on neighboring films must show the covered arcs meeting or overlapping, so no length of weld falls between shots. Divide the circumference into equal segments and mark them before the first exposure.
- **Identification** makes the radiograph a record: job or contract, weld and station number, date, and the radiographer's identity appear on every film — typically with lead characters or flash markings — so any film can be traced to one weld on one day, years later.

A perfect image of an unidentified arc of pipe is scrap. Coverage planning and traceability are as much a part of the exposure technique as the source position.

sections.title: The pipe-shot decision tree
sections.body: Can you get source or film inside? **Shoot single-wall.** No access and OD over about 3.5 in.? **DWSI** — source offset outside, film opposite, interpret the film-side wall, **≥ 3 shots at 120°**. OD 3.5 in. or less? **DWDI elliptical**, **≥ 2 shots at 90°** — or **superimposed, ≥ 3 shots at 60°/120°** when the ellipse will not separate. Every technique: location markers, planned overlap, IQI per the governing code for the wall being viewed.

sections.caption: Technique selection summary for circumferential pipe butt welds.
rows: Plate; vessels; pipe with internal source or film
Pipe > 3.5 in. OD, no internal access
Both walls, separated as an ellipse

figures.title: Pipe weld exposure setups: SW, DWSI, DWDI

interactive.title: Pipe Shot Planner
interactive.intro: You are planning radiography on piping in a fabrication shop with an Ir-192 gamma projector, film cassettes, IQIs, and lead location markers. For each weld, make the call a Level II would make.

steps.prompt: First weld: a circumferential butt weld on 12 in. OD header pipe. The line is closed — no internal access — but film can be wrapped anywhere on the outside. Which technique?
options: Single-wall with the source centered inside the pipe
Double-wall exposure, single-wall viewing: source offset outside one wall, film against the opposite wall
Double-wall double-image elliptical with two exposures
steps.feedback: With no internal access on large-bore pipe, you expose through both walls and interpret only the film-side weld — DWSI. Elliptical double-image work is reserved for small bore, commonly 3.5 in. OD and under.

steps.prompt: How many exposures, minimum, will that 12 in. DWSI weld take for complete coverage, and how are they arranged?
options: One exposure, since the film wraps the full circumference
Two exposures, 180 degrees apart
Three exposures spaced 120 degrees apart, with overlapping coverage shown by location markers
steps.feedback: Each DWSI shot presents only a limited arc at acceptable quality, so codes require at least three exposures at 120-degree spacing, with lead location markers proving overlap between adjacent films.

steps.prompt: Next weld: a butt weld on 2.375 in. OD tubing. Which setup?
options: DWSI with three exposures
DWDI elliptical: source offset from the weld plane so both walls read on one film as an ellipse, at least two exposures 90 degrees apart
A single straight-through exposure viewed once
steps.feedback: At 3.5 in. OD and under, both walls project sharply, so the elliptical double-image technique applies: offset the source, open the weld into an ellipse, and shoot at least twice with 90 degrees of rotation between exposures.

steps.prompt: Your first ellipse attempt comes back with the two root images lying directly on top of each other. What went wrong?
options: The film was too slow for the source activity
The source was aimed straight through the weld plane with no axial offset, so the images superimposed
The IQI was placed on the film side instead of the source side
steps.feedback: The ellipse comes entirely from offsetting the source along the pipe axis: that throws the source-side weld image clear of the film-side image. No offset, no ellipse — you shot a superimposed image by accident.

steps.prompt: Back on the 12 in. DWSI films: the source-side weld image is faintly visible, enlarged and fuzzy, crossing one corner of the film. What do you do?
options: Reject the film — two weld images means a bad setup
Interpret the sharp film-side weld image; the blurred source-side image is expected and is not evaluated, provided it does not interfere
Interpret whichever image shows the IQI better
steps.feedback: In DWSI only the film-side weld is evaluated. The magnified, unsharp source-side image is inherent to the geometry; the offset exists to keep it from interfering with the image you read.

checkQuestions.question: Why do codes direct radiographers to use a single-wall technique whenever it is practical?
options: Single-wall exposures require no image quality indicator
The beam crosses only the thickness being interpreted, giving the best sensitivity and the shortest exposure
Single-wall techniques never require multiple exposures
Double-wall techniques are prohibited on welds
checkQuestions.explanation: With one wall in the beam, exposure is shortest, scatter is lowest, and the IQI and sensitivity requirements apply to exactly the thickness being viewed — the best image quality achievable. Double-wall is an access-driven compromise.

checkQuestions.question: In a double-wall exposure, single-wall viewing (DWSI) shot on 8 in. pipe, which weld image is evaluated?
options: The weld nearest the source, because it receives the most radiation
The weld nearest the film, because it projects sharply with negligible magnification
Both welds equally
Whichever image is darker
checkQuestions.explanation: Only the film-side weld is interpreted in DWSI: being close to the film, its image is sharp. The source-side weld image is magnified and blurred by the geometry and is not evaluated.

checkQuestions.question: What is the purpose of offsetting the source in the elliptical small-bore technique?
options: To shorten the exposure time
To reduce geometric unsharpness of the film-side image
To throw the source-side weld image clear of the film-side image so both walls can be read separately as an ellipse
To eliminate the need for location markers
checkQuestions.explanation: Offsetting the source along the pipe axis displaces the source-side weld image so the weld opens into an ellipse on the film, separating the two wall images for interpretation.

checkQuestions.question: A weld on 2 in. OD pipe is shot with the superimposed double-wall double-image technique. The minimum coverage requirement is typically:
options: One exposure
Two exposures at 90 degrees
Three exposures at 60- or 120-degree increments
Six exposures at 30-degree increments
checkQuestions.explanation: Superimposed DWDI requires at least three exposures rotated 60 or 120 degrees apart so every portion of the weld is presented acceptably; the elliptical variant requires at least two at 90 degrees.

checkQuestions.question: A crew must radiograph a closed 16 in. OD line with film wrapped outside and the source positioned outside the opposite wall. Complete coverage requires at least:
options: One exposure, because the film wraps the pipe
Two exposures, because each film sees half the circumference
Three exposures spaced 120 degrees apart, with overlap proven by location markers
Four exposures, one per quadrant, with no overlap needed
checkQuestions.explanation: This is DWSI: each shot yields acceptable quality over only a limited arc, so a minimum of three exposures at 120-degree spacing is required, with location markers demonstrating overlapping coverage.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Radiography Technique Course 6.1–6.2.3
Weld radiography practice per codes such as ASME Section V (single-wall/double-wall techniques, coverage, and IQI selection)
General piping radiography practice: DWSI ≥ 3 exposures at 120°; elliptical ≥ 2 at 90°; superimposed ≥ 3 at 60°/120°
